text.title: SERP Intent Classification Rubric Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every SERP Intent Classification Rubric application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Primary intent MUST be one of exactly four classes: Informational (I), Comparative (C), Transactional (T), Navigational (N). Custom classes are rejected.
rule.rationale: Open-ended intent vocabularies become noise; a closed set is the only way to keep the rubric repeatable across taggers.

rule.statement: Every classification MUST cite ≥2 independent SERP signals (page-type distribution, SERP features, related searches, snippet word patterns).
rule.rationale: Gut-feel intent tags are wrong ~30% of the time on long-tail queries; SERP-evidence anchoring makes classification falsifiable.

rule.statement: A secondary intent MAY be tagged ONLY if ≥30% of top-10 results clearly serve a different class.
rule.rationale: Auto-adding secondaries dilutes the contract for drafters.

rule.statement: When two distinct intent classes share comparable SERP weight, recommend SPLIT — produce two briefs, not a hedged 'covers both'.
rule.rationale: Hedged content serves neither audience; splitting at classification time is cheaper than rewriting.

rule.statement: If SERP top-10 has <3 dominant page types AND no clear SERP features, tag intent=AMBIGUOUS and block content production pending user research.
rule.rationale: Drafting on ambiguous SERPs is gambling; the right move is research.

rule.statement: Navigational MUST distinguish brand-N (specific company/product) from category-N (generic 'best CRM'); mis-tagging generic queries as brand-N is rejected.
rule.rationale: Generic queries surfacing brand pages are Comparative or Transactional, not Navigational.
